1: Theme — Prostitute's wages on the threshing floor — covenant infidelity pictured as economic transaction, trading worship for agricultural profit. Against this, Matt 6:24: 'You cannot serve God and money.' Jesus names the same impossible double allegiance, and the entire sermon on the mount dismantles the transactional approach to God.
2: Type — Threshing floor and wine press failing to provide — the agricultural means of life withdraw from those who have abandoned covenant. John 15:5: 'Apart from me you can do nothing.' Christ is the True Vine from whom all fruit proceeds; those cut off from the source find exactly what Hosea's Israel finds — the harvests fail.
3: Type — Return to Egypt, eat ritually unclean food — exile as new-anti-Exodus, reversing Sinai's distinctions. Christ accomplishes a greater new exodus: Mark 7:19 — he 'declared all foods clean,' resolving the clean/unclean distinction at its root by addressing the heart rather than the food. The clean/unclean reversal of the exile is reversed again in Christ.
4: Type — Offerings like mourners' bread, defiling all who eat — sacrifice that conveys not blessing but contamination. Heb 9:13-14: 'If the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sanctify for the purification of the flesh, how much more will the blood of Christ… purify our conscience.' Hosea's defiling bread stands against Christ's bread of life that truly cleanses.
5: Type — Day of the appointed festival — the festal calendar of Israel (Passover, Firstfruits, Weeks, Tabernacles, Atonement) pointed ahead. Col 2:16-17: 'these are a shadow of things to come; the reality belongs to Christ.' Every appointed festival anticipated the one who fulfilled its significance: the Passover lamb, the firstfruits offering, the Day of Atonement scapegoat.
6: Type — Fleeing to Egypt, Memphis burying them — the anti-exodus: Israel returning to the house of bondage. The Holy Family's flight to Egypt (Matt 2:13-15) reverses this exactly: instead of Israel going to Egypt and dying, the Christ-child enters Egypt and comes out alive, so that Hosea 11:1's 'out of Egypt I called my son' is fulfilled in the one who repeats the pattern victoriously.
7: Type — The prophet is a fool, the inspired man is mad — rejection of God's messengers reaching its pattern. Luke 13:34: 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those sent to her!' Jesus stands at the end of this prophetic line — the last and greatest messenger rejected as a fool and madman (John 10:20: 'He has a demon and is out of his mind').
8: Theme — Ephraim's watchman standing beside God — the prophetic office as watchman/guardian that has become a trap instead. John 10:11: 'I am the good shepherd; the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' Against the false watchman who traps, Christ is the true shepherd who guards and gives his life.
9: Direct — 'As in the days of Gibeah' — the Gibeah atrocity (Judg 19-20) as paradigm of Israel's worst corruption. 1 Cor 10:6-11: Paul explicitly treats Israel's wilderness and pre-exile failures as 'types' written for our instruction. The Gibeah pattern functions exactly as Paul describes: 'these things happened as examples, and they were written for our admonition.'
10: Direct — 'I found Israel like grapes in the wilderness' — the early delight that gave way to Baal-peor's consecration to shame. John 15:1-5: 'I am the True Vine.' Jesus reconstitutes the Israel-as-vine image: where Hosea's Israel was first delight then corruption, Jesus is the vine whose fruit is permanent when the branches abide in him. The Baal-peor consecration is undone by baptism into Christ.
11: Direct — 'Their glory will fly away like a bird — no birth, no pregnancy, no conception' — Israel's fruitfulness withdrawn. John 3:3: 'Unless one is born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God.' The new birth Christ brings — spiritual conception and regeneration — is the direct answer to the threatened barrenness here. What Hosea's judgment withdraws, Christ's Spirit restores through new creation.
12: Theme — 'I will bereave them until not one is left' — divine removal of children as judgment. Matt 18:14: 'It is not the will of your Father in heaven that one of these little ones should perish.' The Father's will is precisely the opposite of the judgment Hosea announces — not because judgment is avoided but because Christ receives it on behalf of the children.
13: Theme — Ephraim in a pleasant place like Tyre, bringing children out to be slain — fruitful positioning that ends in destruction. Heb 11:10: Abraham looked forward to 'the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God.' The earthly pleasant places of Israel fail; the city built by God endures. Citizenship in Christ's city is the security that Tyre-like prosperity cannot provide.
14: Type — 'Give them a womb that miscarries and breasts that are dry' — the prophet prays that Israel have no descendants to suffer judgment. The entire barrenness-to-fruitfulness trajectory of the Bible (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth) is finally resolved in Mary, whose conception is itself miraculous, and in the spiritual offspring of the cross: Isa 53:10 ('he will see his offspring and prolong his days').
15: Direct — 'I will drive them out of my house; I will no longer love them' — expulsion from covenant presence. John 6:37: 'Whoever comes to me I will never drive away.' The divine expulsion of Hosea's judgment is answered by Christ's absolute promise of reception. The 'no longer love' of covenant rupture is answered by the love that is permanent: John 13:1, 'having loved his own… he loved them to the end.'
16: Type — Struck down, root dried up, children put to death — total destruction of the covenant line. John 15:2: 'Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away; every branch that does bear fruit he prunes.' The same vine/fruit imagery: fruitless branches are cut. But for those who remain in Christ, fruitfulness is guaranteed — the dried root becomes a branch in the True Vine.
17: Theme — 'They will become wanderers among the nations' — the Cain-like sentence of exile. John 11:52: Christ dies 'to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad.' The scattering among the nations, which began here and intensified in 70 AD, is precisely what the gospel gathering reverses — the ingathering of the diaspora into the body of Christ.
